The Lord says that even Moses and Samuel must have pleaded in vain. Presenting this as an example shows that they do not, and that saints in heaven do not pray for saints on earth. The Jews were condemned to different kinds of misery by the righteous judgment of God, and the remnant would be driven away like chaff into captivity. Then the populous city became desolate. Bad examples and misused authority often produce fatal effects, even after people are dead or have repented of their crimes; this should make all greatly dread being the cause of sin in others.

Jeremiah met with much contempt and reproach when they ought to have blessed him and God for him. It is a great and sufficient support for the people of God that however troublesome their way may be, it will be well with them in their final days. God turns to the people. Will even the most determined of their efforts be able to contend with the counsel of God or with the army of the Chaldeans? Let them hear their doom. The enemy will treat the prophet well, but the people who had great estates would be treated harshly. All parts of the country added to the national guilt; and let each take shame to itself.

It is a comfort that we have a God whose knowledge of all things we may appeal to. Jeremiah pleads with God for mercy and relief against his enemies, persecutors, and slanderers. It will be a comfort to God’s ministers when people despise them if they have the testimony of their own consciences. But he complains that he found little pleasure in his work. Some good people lose much of the pleasantness of religion because of the fretfulness and uneasiness of their natural temper, which they indulge. The Lord called the prophet to cease from his distrust and to return to his work. If he attended thereto, he might be assured the Lord would deliver him from his enemies. Those who are with God and faithful to him, he will deliver from trouble or carry through it. Many things appear frightful that do not at all hurt a real believer in Christ.
